text: The patient is positioned in a supine split-leg position. Six laparoscopic ports are visible on the abdomen. The surgeon creates a window into the lesser sac and begins mobilizing the colon, carefully preserving the gastroepiploic artery. The hepatic flexure of the colon is dissected and taken down.

The surgeon bluntly separates the attachments of the transverse colon mesentery from the duodenum, exposing the duodenum. The transverse colon is mobilized to the right and left of the gastrocolic trunk area, facilitating the exposure and dissection of the gastrocolic trunk of Henle, which is then ligated and divided.

The surgeon creates a window anterior to the hepatoduodenal ligament to identify the upper edge of the duodenum and the pylorus. A stapler is placed two to three centimeters distal to the duodenum for division. The gastroduodenal artery is ligated and divided, exposing the area of the hepatic artery, which is then identified.

The surgeon excises the anterior hepatic artery lymph node, enhancing the visibility of the hepatic artery and the origin of the gastroduodenal artery. The window between these arteries is exposed, and the portal vein at the superior edge of the pancreas within the hepatoduodenal ligament is visualized. A plane is developed around the gastroduodenal artery, and a silk suture is passed.

The surgeon ligates and divides the artery while carefully avoiding injury to the hepatic artery. The dissection proceeds by entering the plane between the portal vein and the common bile duct. The common bile duct is dissected circumferentially, with the option to ligate and divide it at this stage or later. The dissection continues towards the cystic duct and gallbladder, with clear exposure of the cystic duct and Calot's triangle.

The surgeon focuses on separating the neck of the pancreas from the superior mesenteric portal vein trunk. The inferior edge of the pancreas is identified, and the serosal layer overlaying it is opened. The area of the superior mesenteric vein adjacent to the pancreas is exposed. An initial window is created inferiorly using careful blunt dissection, which is then enlarged on both sides for direct visualization. Small vessels are ligated with ultrasonic shears to ensure a bloodless dissection. Finger-type dissection is used to complete the 360-degree dissection of the neck, with the parenchymal division planned for later.

The surgeon approaches the ligament of Treitz, initially from the left, to take down adhesions. The jejunum is divided using a co-care maneuver. The third portion of the duodenum is exposed and retracted anteriorly. Posterior serosal attachments to the retroperitoneum are divided by creating a window, followed by sharp division of the serosal layer and careful blunt dissection to tease away retroperitoneal attachments. The inferior vena cava is exposed, and posterior attachments between the head of the pancreas and the retroperitoneum are divided.

The surgeon identifies and divides the attachments of the third and fourth portions of the duodenum behind the superior mesenteric vessels. The duodenum is traced towards the ligament of Treitz, which is taken down from the right side. The jejunum is identified and brought behind the superior mesenteric vessels, with its relationship to the superior mesenteric vein visible. A window is created for jejunal division, and a stapler is used to align and divide the jejunum. The pancreatic neck, previously separated from the superior mesenteric vein portal vein trunk, is divided using ultrasonic shears, ensuring hemostasis. The splenic vein is identified.

The surgeon identifies the pancreatic duct and divides it with scissors, making the cut slightly to the right of the parenchymal transection. Ultrasonic shears are then used to complete the transection of the pancreatic parenchyma. The superior mesenteric portal vein trunk is visible. The common bile duct, previously dissected in 360 degrees, is divided using a bulldog clamp. The anterior wall is opened first, followed by the posterior wall, which is cut slightly longer to aid in future anastomosis.

The surgeon begins dissection of the uncinate process, ensuring vascular control by passing vessel loops around the portal vein and superior mesenteric vein. The splenic vein portal vein junction is exposed and surrounded with a vessel loop. Bipolar and ultrasonic shear dissection techniques are used to control small branch vessels between the superior mesenteric vessels and the uncinate process. The surgeon retracts the uncinate process to the right while the assistant retracts the superior mesenteric vein and artery to the left, allowing clear visualization of the superior mesenteric artery. Larger branches between the superior mesenteric portal vein trunk and the uncinate process are ligated with clips before division. Laparoscopic magnification aids in the precise excision of the uncinate process extensions.

The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears for finer dissection posterior to the superior mesenteric artery, achieving excellent visualization through laparoscopic exposure. Right-sided retraction creates tension, allowing lymph nodes behind the superior mesenteric vessels to be included in the specimen. The specimen is completely separated, and any remaining fibrolymphatic tissue attached to the superior mesenteric artery is excised. The specimen is placed in an endoscopic retrieval bag and removed from the abdominal cavity through an enlarged incision site.

The surgeon begins an end-to-side biliary-enteric anastomosis with the patient in a supine split-leg position, standing to the right. Right-sided ports are used for triangulation during the procedure. The proximal end of the divided jejunum is brought behind the superior mesenteric vessels, ensuring no kinks or tension at the anastomosis site. The bulldog clamp on the bile duct is removed, and an opening is made in the jejunum to match the duct size. The seromuscular layer is opened, and the mucosa is everted to facilitate the anastomosis. Stitches are placed under direct visualization, aligning the duct corners with the bowel, using a three-millimeter tip needle driver with a five-millimeter shaft for precise needle placement. The posterior layer is completed with care to avoid slack in the suture.

The surgeon begins the anterior layer of the anastomosis with an outside-in, inside-out stitch, ensuring alignment with the posterior layer. The assistant maintains tension on the suture for exposure. The completed anastomosis is shown without tension. The surgeon constructs a two-layer ductal mucosa pancreaticojejunostomy, starting with the posterior layer between the pancreas and jejunum. A running monofilament suture is used, and the pancreatic duct is probed with a pediatric feeding tube. An opening in the jejunum is created using ultrasonic energy, and interrupted stitches are placed for the duct-to-mucosa anastomosis. The anterior layer is completed with a running suture, inverting the pancreatic edge and covering it with the seromuscular layer of the jejunum.

A closed suction drain is positioned near the anastomosis site. The surgeon proceeds with gastrointestinal reconstruction, confirming good vascular supply around the pylorus and proximal duodenum. Anchoring stitches are placed at the old ligament of Treitz area, and an antecolic postpyloric duodenal jejunostomy is performed. The posterior seromuscular layer is created between the jejunum and duodenum edge using a running absorbable suture. The postpyloric duodenum is opened longitudinally, and the pylorus is dilated. The jejunum is opened parallel but smaller than the duodenum. A 360-degree anastomosis is formed with two running sutures, incorporating all bowel layers. The anterior layer is completed with smaller bites, finishing with an inverting seromuscular stitch. The anastomosis is patent and tension-free.
